title: Foundations: Soteriology – Preservation of the Saints and Glorification (Part 1)
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro
source_files: FOUNDATIONS - Lesson 12 - Soteriology_Salvation - Preservation of the Saints and Glorification.txt

outline.title: The Biblical Basis for Preservation: Eternal Life
subpoints: John 3:16, 3:36, and 5:24 all describe eternal life as an immediate possession of the believer
Life given by Jesus is forever by definition—not temporarily eternal
The perseverance of the saints depends on the preservation by God, not human willpower
Colossians portrays believers as doubly held—in both the Son's hand and the Father's hand

outline.title: Addressing Common Objections to Perseverance
subpoints: Hebrews 6 and similar passages warn those who appear to have been saved but have not truly believed
1 John 2:19 explains that those who fall away prove they were never truly part of the elect
Matthew 7:21-23 describes those who had external gifts and works but no true saving relationship with Christ
The Holy Spirit's sealing (Ephesians 1:13-14) and God's ongoing work (Philippians 1:6) guarantee perseverance

outline.title: The "Golden Chain" of Salvation
subpoints: Romans 8:28-30 presents the unbroken chain: predestined, called, justified, glorified
The grammatical repetition of 'whom' and 'these' shows the same group persists from beginning to end
The use of the prophetic past tense speaks of glorification as already certain from God's perspective
This closed logical system admits no additions or subtractions

outline.title: Introduction to Glorification: The Intermediate State
subpoints: At death, the believer's soul is made perfect and immediately passes into glory
The state of the souls in heaven now is better than life on earth (Philippians 1:23)
However, the intermediate state is not the ultimate goal—the soul without the body is 'naked' (2 Corinthians 5)
The saints in heaven long for something beyond the intermediate state: vindication and resurrection

outline.title: The Three Tenses of Salvation and the Christian's Ultimate Hope
subpoints: Past tense: the believer has been saved from the guilt and penalty of sin (Ephesians 2:8)
Present tense: the believer is being saved from the power of sin (1 Corinthians 1:18)
Future tense: the believer will be saved from the very presence of sin (Romans 5:9-10)
The Christian's ultimate hope is not death but the bodily resurrection at Christ's return

summary:
In this twelfth lesson of the Foundations series, Pastor Tito Lyro continues the study of soteriology by first completing the topic of preservation of the saints and then introducing glorification. He establishes the biblical case for the believer's eternal security by noting that John 3:16, 3:36, and 5:24 all describe eternal life as an immediate present possession—not something that can be revoked. The life Christ gives is definitionally forever; a 'temporarily eternal' life would be a contradiction.

Pastor Lyro addresses common objections to perseverance with care. Hebrews 6 is explained as describing those who had external exposure to the gospel and its blessings but were never truly regenerate. 1 John 2:19 clarifies that those who depart from the faith demonstrate they were never truly part of the elect. Matthew 7:21-23 shows that external gifts and works, even in Jesus' name, are no substitute for a genuine saving relationship. The Holy Spirit's sealing (Ephesians 1:13-14) and God's ongoing sanctifying work (Philippians 1:6) are further confirmations that the elect will be preserved to the end.

The lesson then turns to glorification, introducing it via the three tenses of salvation: the believer has been saved (justification), is being saved (sanctification), and will be saved (glorification). Pastor Lyro draws from Romans 8:28-30's 'golden chain' to show that the same group predestined by God is the same group ultimately glorified—no one is added or subtracted along the way. The prophetic past tense used for glorification in that passage confirms its certainty from God's perspective.

Finally, he distinguishes the intermediate state (the soul's presence with God after death) from the ultimate glory of bodily resurrection. While heaven now is better than earth, it is an incomplete state—the soul without the body is described in 2 Corinthians 5 as 'naked.' The saints in Revelation 6 cry out for something beyond the intermediate state. The Christian's true hope is not merely to die and go to heaven, but to be resurrected at Christ's return.
transcript:
## Introduction and Review

The rest of us will continue on our series on foundations of the Christian faith as we started a few months ago. And the Lord willing we may finish today. We have been talking about doctrines that are foundation of the Christian faith. And since I believe that the Bible teaches a reformed view of the Christian faith, then these foundational doctrines are going to be in line with the reformed faith as well.

And lately we've been looking at God's plan of salvation from beginning to end, redemption accomplished and redemption applied. And God's plan of salvation takes a person from being dead in his or her sins and brings him or her to glory. We have spent the last few weeks studying the journey from simply dead to righteous of life. And today, we are right at the destiny of the child of God, which is glorification, the final act on behalf of the believer.

Just as a way of review, we've been looking at the doctrine of salvation, or to use the theological term, soteriology. We've been looking at the order in which these things happen—the ordo salutis, the logical order of salvation, not a time order. Some of these things are happening at the same time. Today we're going to look at the preservation of the saints and glorification.

And as you remember from last week, we're looking at the preservation of the saints, because sanctification is just that—as we grow progressively to become more and more like Jesus Christ, God is persevering, preserving us to the end.

## The Biblical Basis for Preservation: Eternal Life

Grab your Bible or a device and turn to John chapter 3, as we look at the scriptural basis for the preservation and perseverance of the saints. I want to start by looking at what the Bible teaches concerning eternal life.

John 3:16: "For God so loved the world that He gave His only begotten Son, that whoever believes in Him should not perish but have everlasting life."

Verse 36 of the same chapter: "He who believes in the Son has everlasting life; and he who does not believe the Son shall not see life, but the wrath of God abides on him." Again, the idea of having eternal life as an immediate consequence of faith in Jesus Christ.

Chapter 5 verse 24, Jesus is speaking: "Most assuredly, I say to you, he who hears My word and believes in Him who sent Me has everlasting life, and shall not come into judgment, but has passed from death into life."

You notice that in both verse 36 and in 5:24 the Bible says that the believer has eternal life—not something that is in his future, not something that he gets if he arrives at the end. At the moment one believed in Jesus, he has eternal life. And it's not a big comfort that you have eternal life now but in the next minute you may not have it. If that were the case, then you didn't really have it a minute ago. There's no comfort in that, and God means it in the Bible as a comfort. The life that is given by Jesus is forever—it is not a temporary forever life; life is a forever-forever life.

## Addressing Common Objections to Perseverance

Now, what about Hebrews 6 and passages like it that seem to teach that a Christian can lose their salvation? The people described in Hebrews 6 are described as having been "enlightened," having "tasted the heavenly gift," being "partakers of the Holy Spirit."

All these things are true of those who are in the outer circle of the church, who are hearing the Word, experiencing community, even receiving gifts of the Spirit without being truly regenerated. They have external exposure to all the blessings of the gospel. And the warning is that if these people turn away from the light they've received, it becomes very difficult for them to be brought to repentance—because they've been hardened by the very gifts they abused.

1 John 2:19 gives us the key: "They went out from us, but they were not of us; for if they had been of us, they would have continued with us; but they went out that they might be made manifest, that none of them were of us." Those who leave prove that they were never truly part of the elect.

Matthew 7:21-23: "Not everyone who says to Me, 'Lord, Lord,' shall enter the kingdom of heaven… Many will say to Me in that day, 'Lord, Lord, have we not prophesied in Your name…?' And then I will declare to them, 'I never knew you; depart from Me, you who practice lawlessness.'" These people had gifts, they had works, but they never had a true saving relationship with Christ.

Ephesians 1:13-14 tells us that the Holy Spirit seals the elect until the day of redemption. Philippians 1:6: "He who has begun a good work in you will complete it until the day of Jesus Christ."

And you can see that the perseverance of the saints ultimately depends on the preservation by God, not on the believer's own effort. Colossians shows us the wonderful picture that we are doubly held by God—we are in Christ's hand, and the Father's hand is around Christ. In order for someone to take a true believer away from God, they would have to pry the hand of the Father off and pry the hand of the Son. That seems to be a very arrogant thought.

## The Golden Chain of Salvation

Romans 8:28-30 presents what has been called the golden chain of salvation: "Moreover whom He predestined, these He also called; whom He called, these He also justified; and whom He justified, these He also glorified." You might say, all these are past tenses—is glorification already complete? That is the prophetic past tense, in which the speaker is speaking from the perspective of the end, looking back at history. It doesn't mean that we have been glorified already, but in the prophetic perspective it is seen as already done.

And if you look at the words "whom" and "these"—they are grammatically the same group of people throughout the verse. There is no other possible interpretation grammatically. It is a closed system: nobody is added, nobody is taken out. The same group that starts at the first "whom" reaches the last "these."

## Introduction to Glorification: The Three Tenses of Salvation

We can think of glorification in three tenses of salvation itself. The Bible speaks about the past tense of salvation: the Christian has been saved from the guilt and the penalty of sin. Ephesians 2:8: "For by grace you have been saved."

We can think of salvation in the present tense: the Christian is being saved from the power of sin. 1 Corinthians 1:18: "For the message of the cross is foolishness to those who are perishing, but to us who are being saved it is the power of God."

And then we can think of salvation in the future tense: the Christian will be completely saved someday from the very presence of sin. Romans 5:9-10: "Much more then, having now been justified by His blood, we shall be saved from wrath through Him. For if when we were enemies we were reconciled to God through the death of His Son, much more, having been reconciled, we shall be saved by His life."

So we have these three tenses of salvation: justification at one point, sanctification in the present, and glorification as the last one. And that's what we come to at this point.

## The Intermediate State and the Soul After Death

Westminster Shorter Catechism Question 37 says that the souls of believers at their death are made perfect and do immediately pass into glory. That's true—the moment a believer dies, his or her soul is made perfect and passes into glory. And it is also true that the state of believers in heaven is a more blessed state than the state of believers who are alive on earth.

We see that in Philippians where Paul says: "For to me, to live is Christ, and to die is gain… having a desire to depart and be with Christ, which is far better." So Paul describes life of the soul in heaven as far better than the life of the Christian right now here on earth.

But here is the part we don't usually think about: heaven now is not the best and most glorious state. Heaven now is not where we are actually looking forward to ultimately. The period between the death of the believers and the resurrection at the return of Christ is called the intermediate state—and it is just that: intermediate, meaning we are trying to get somewhere else beyond that.

Paul speaks of this period as desirable only because it is temporary. In 2 Corinthians 5: "For we know that if our earthly house, this tent, is destroyed, we have a building from God, a house not made with hands, eternal in the heavens. For in this we groan, earnestly desiring to be clothed with our habitation which is from heaven… that mortality may be swallowed up by life."

He talks about the disembodied state of a human as a "naked" state—an incomplete state, not really what we're made for. Heaven now is better than here, but the future resurrected state is better than heaven.

The saints in heaven, in Revelation 6, have a desire for something more: "How long, O Lord, holy and true, until You judge and avenge our blood on those who dwell on the earth?" They are waiting for something. There's something that the souls in heaven are desiring that goes beyond that state.

So Christians are not ultimately looking for death. Death is not the ultimate experience for which a Christian should long. Our blessed hope is the glorious appearing of our great God and Savior Jesus Christ, at whose coming those who have died in the faith and those who are alive at the time of this coming will be changed. We're looking through death to the resurrection—that's our ultimate hope.

## Closing Prayer

We will finish the topic of glorification next time. Father, having thank You that You are a good God to us and thank You that we know that the Lord Jesus Christ is coming back for us. We pray that in light of that blessed hope we live rightly now and encourage one another, looking forward to the day in which we're going to see Him as He is and we shall be like Him. We pray these things in Jesus' name. Amen.
